sentences.base_language: I work as a programmer in a tech company.
sentences.target_language: Trabajo como programador en una empresa de tecnología.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Trabajar como** + profession = to work as: **trabajo como programador**, **trabaja como enfermera**.
This is an alternative to **ser**: **soy programador** and **trabajo como programador** are both natural.
**La empresa** = company/firm — also: **la compañía**, **el negocio** (business). **De tecnología** = tech (of technology).

sentences.base_language: I start work at nine in the morning.
sentences.target_language: Empiezo a trabajar a las nueve de la mañana.
sentences.grammarNote: Empezar is e→ie: empiezo, empiezas, empieza, empezamos, empezáis, empiezan. 'Empezar a + infinitive' = to start doing something: 'empiezo a trabajar' (I start working), 'empezamos a estudiar' (we start studying).

sentences.base_language: My boss calls a team meeting at ten.
sentences.target_language: Mi jefe convoca una reunión de equipo a las diez.
sentences.grammarNote:
**El jefe / la jefa** = boss/chief — gender forms differ.
**Convocar** = to call/convene (a meeting) — more formal than **tener**: **convocar una reunión** (to call a meeting), **convocar una huelga** (to call a strike).
**A las diez** = at ten — use **a las** for all hours except one (**a la una**).

'Hablar de' = to talk about. 'Hablamos del proyecto' = We talk about the project. 'Del' is the mandatory contraction of 'de + el'. You also hear 'pensar en' (to think about), 'depender de' (to depend on).

sentences.base_language: I work hard and finish the task before midday.
sentences.target_language: Trabajo duro y termino la tarea antes del mediodía.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Trabajar duro** = to work hard — **duro** here is an adverb (invariable). Also common: **trabajar mucho** (to work a lot).
**Antes del mediodía** — **antes de** + noun/infinitive = before: **antes del trabajo** (before work), **antes de salir** (before leaving).
**Del** = de + el (mandatory contraction).

sentences.base_language: I take a short break and eat lunch.
sentences.target_language: Tomo un descanso corto y almuerzo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tomar un descanso** = to take a break — **tomar** is used in many fixed expressions.
**Almorzar** = to have lunch (stem-changing verb: o→ue): **almuerzo, almuerzas, almuerza, almorzamos...**
Note the meal vocabulary: **desayunar** (to have breakfast), **almorzar** (to have lunch), **cenar** (to have dinner) — all -ar verbs used directly without a direct object.

sentences.base_language: In the afternoon, I help a coworker with his work.
sentences.target_language: Por la tarde, ayudo a un compañero de trabajo con su tarea.
sentences.grammarNote: The 'personal a' is required before a specific person as a direct object: 'ayudo a un compañero' (I help a coworker), 'llamo a mi jefe' (I call my boss), 'veo a mi amigo' (I see my friend). It doesn't translate into English.

sentences.target_language: Termino de trabajar a las seis.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Terminar de + infinitive** = to finish doing something: **termino de trabajar** (I finish working), **terminé de comer** (I finished eating).
Contrast with **terminar + noun**: **termino el informe** (I finish the report — direct object).
**A las seis** = at six o'clock — time expressions with **a las** for hours 2–12.

Both **cansado** (tired) and **satisfecho** (satisfied) use **estar** — they describe current states, not permanent traits.
**Estoy cansado** = I'm tired right now (not **soy cansado**, which would imply being a chronically tiring person).
**Satisfecho** is the past participle of **satisfacer** (to satisfy) used as an adjective — it agrees in gender: **cansada / satisfecha** for a woman.
